_consumer: src/app/components/TreasureHunt.tsx — challenges drive the in-context hunt. Schema mirrors the HuntChallenge interface. Content aligned to docs/Conceptual Design/Experience-Spec.md (TREASURE HUNT section).

challenges.title: When the First Stone Was Laid
challenges.description: Standing in front of the Duomo, you are looking at six centuries of construction. Tell us when it all began.
challenges.question: In what year did construction of the Duomo begin?

feedback.correct: Correct — 1386, under Archbishop Antonio da Saluzzo. Construction continued for nearly six centuries, spanning Milan's evolution from medieval commune to modern metropolis.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite. The first stone was laid in 1386, and the cathedral took almost 600 years to finish.

challenges.arHint: Stand facing the cathedral's main facade — the stonework you see spans the Birth, Crown, and Modern periods.

challenges.title: The Glass-Painter's Happy Accident
challenges.description: Near the Duomo's north door, an audio clue plays the sound of a 16th-century glass-painter at work. Listen, then answer.
challenges.question: What did a Belgian glass-painter accidentally invent at this cathedral in 1574?

feedback.correct: Exactly — risotto alla milanese. Legend says a Flemish glass-painter working on the Duomo's windows loved saffron so much that in 1574 his assistant tinted a wedding feast's rice golden as a joke. The saffron risotto stuck.
feedback.incorrect: It's risotto alla milanese — Milan's golden saffron rice. The story goes that a glass-painter's love of saffron pigment turned a 1574 wedding feast's rice bright yellow.

challenges.arHint: Face the Duomo's north door and tap the glowing audio marker to hear the glass-painter's workshop.

challenges.title: The Golden Lady on Top
challenges.description: At the very top of the Duomo's tallest spire stands the golden symbol of Milan. Go outside, look up, and capture her.
challenges.photoPrompt: Point your camera up at the central spire and capture La Madonnina.
challenges.verifierSubject: Gilded statue of the Virgin Mary on top of a Gothic spire

feedback.correct: Well done! La Madonnina stands 108.5 m above Piazza del Duomo. Placed in 1774, she is gilded copper and 4.16 m tall. By tradition no building in Milan rose above her — a rule respected until the skyscrapers of the 1950s.
feedback.incorrect: The statue is called La Madonnina. Stand in the piazza and point your camera up at the tallest central spire.

challenges.arHint: Point your camera at the top of the Duomo's central facade and look for the glowing spire marker.

challenges.title: How High She Stands
challenges.description: You have seen La Madonnina crowning the cathedral. Now — exactly how far above the square does she stand?
challenges.question: How high above the ground does the Madonnina stand?

feedback.correct: Right — 108.5 metres. The gilded Madonnina has crowned the Duomo's tallest spire since 1774, and for almost two centuries no Milanese building was allowed to rise higher.
feedback.incorrect: She stands 108.5 metres above Piazza del Duomo (108 is close enough). For nearly 200 years that was the unofficial height limit for the whole city.

challenges.arHint: Look up at the central spire — the marker shows how far the statue sits above the piazza.

challenges.title: The Architect of the Court
challenges.description: One neoclassical architect gave Milan both its royal palace facade and its great opera house. Name him.
challenges.question: Which architect designed both Palazzo Reale and La Scala?

feedback.correct: Correct — Giuseppe Piermarini. The Habsburg court architect rebuilt Palazzo Reale in 1773–78 and designed the Teatro alla Scala, opened in 1778, shaping neoclassical Milan.
feedback.incorrect: The architect was Giuseppe Piermarini. He rebuilt Palazzo Reale (1773–78) and designed La Scala (1778) for Milan's Habsburg court.

challenges.arHint: Stand facing Palazzo Reale — Piermarini's calm neoclassical front faces the piazza.
challenges.location: Palazzo Reale — main facade on Piazza del Duomo

challenges.title: The Floor That Brings Luck
challenges.description: Hidden in the Galleria's mosaic floor is a tradition that visitors travel from all over the world to perform.
challenges.photoPrompt: Find the bull mosaic in the Galleria floor and photograph it.
challenges.verifierSubject: Floor mosaic of a bull with crossed flag at centre of a Galleria floor

feedback.correct: Well spotted! The bull is the emblem of Turin, one of four city crests in the floor. Spinning on its heel three times is said to bring good luck — the mosaic is worn hollow from centuries of heel-spins.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the centre of the octagonal crossing — the bull mosaic sits among the crests of Milan, Turin, Florence and Rome.

challenges.arHint: Enter AR view and look for the highlighted circle at the centre of the octagonal crossing.
challenges.location: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II — central octagon

challenges.title: The Architect and His Arcade
challenges.description: The Galleria's designer never saw it fully open. His story ends on the scaffolding of his own triumphal arch.
challenges.question: Who fell from the scaffolding of his own arcade in 1877?

feedback.correct: Correct — Giuseppe Mengoni. On 30 December 1877, two days before the inauguration, he fell from the scaffolding of the triumphal arch facing the Duomo and died on the spot, aged 47.
feedback.incorrect: It was Giuseppe Mengoni, the Galleria's architect. He fell from the scaffolding of his triumphal arch on 30 December 1877, days before the grand opening.

challenges.arHint: Look up at the iron-and-glass dome — the four arms of Mengoni's arcade meet above this octagon.
challenges.location: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II — central octagon

challenges.title: The Room Left in Ruins
challenges.description: One hall of Palazzo Reale was wrecked by the 1943 bombing and deliberately never fully restored. Name it.
challenges.question: What room of Palazzo Reale was destroyed and never fully restored?
acceptedAnswers: sala delle cariatidi (hall of the caryatids)

feedback.correct: Exactly — the Sala delle Cariatidi. The former royal ballroom was gutted by fire after the August 1943 bombing and deliberately left scarred as a permanent war memorial.
feedback.incorrect: It's the Sala delle Cariatidi — the royal ballroom, ruined in the 1943 bombing and kept unrestored as a memorial.

challenges.arHint: Face Palazzo Reale and activate AR — the 1943 layer reveals the bomb damage to the hall.

challenges.title: The Ballroom That Was Never Rebuilt
challenges.description: Inside Palazzo Reale, find the doorway into the bombed ballroom that has been left in ruins since 1943.
challenges.photoPrompt: Find the entrance to the Sala delle Cariatidi inside Palazzo Reale.
challenges.verifierSubject: Doorway sign reading 'Sala delle Cariatidi' or a view of damaged neoclassical columns

feedback.correct: Found it! Beyond this doorway the Sala delle Cariatidi stands as it was left after 1943 — scarred neoclassical caryatids under a lost ceiling. Today Palazzo Reale is one of Italy's most-visited exhibition venues.
feedback.incorrect: Look for the doorway sign reading 'Sala delle Cariatidi'. Staff at the entrance can point you to it.

challenges.arHint: Stand at the Palazzo entrance and follow the AR markers toward the Sala delle Cariatidi.
challenges.location: Palazzo Reale — Sala delle Cariatidi entrance

challenges.title: The Day Milan Was Free
challenges.description: A 20-second video clue plays archive footage of a celebrating crowd in Piazza del Duomo. Watch, then answer.
challenges.question: What date is being celebrated in this footage?

feedback.correct: Correct — 25 April 1945, Italy's Liberation Day, marking the end of Nazi occupation and Fascist rule. Milan still fills Piazza del Duomo every year to remember it.
feedback.incorrect: The footage marks 25 April 1945 — Liberation Day — when Milan was freed at the end of the Second World War.

challenges.arHint: Face the centre of Piazza del Duomo and tap the 1945 video marker to play the Liberation footage.
